title: Local Electroneutrality Violation as a Universal Constraint in Confined Electrolytes
abstract: We show that finite-size violations of local electroneutrality in confined electrolytes are governed by the topology of the confining domain, yielding a universal hierarchy of deviations across spherical, cylindrical, and planar geometries. Within Poisson-Boltzmann theory, we introduce an electroneutrality deviation ratio that quantifies how global electrostatic constrains associated with compacness and boundary multiplicity modify charge balance inside confined domains. Although electroneutrality is asymptotically restored in all geometries, finite-size deviations are strongest in compact spherical cavities, weaker in cylindrical confinement, and weakest in planar slits. These results identify topology as the structural origin of confinement-induced charge redistribution and stablish the violation of local electroneutrality as global constraint underlying phenomena such as overcharging anf charge reversal, demostrating that confinement-not local-not local geometric details-controls the emergence of these effects.
summary: This paper investigates how the shape and topology of a container affect the distribution of ions in an electrolyte. It demonstrates that the degree to which local electroneutrality is violated depends on the global structure of the space, specifically following a hierarchy from spheres to cylinders to planes. This finding suggests that phenomena like charge reversal are driven by global topological constraints rather than just local curvature. The study provides a unified principle for understanding charge redistribution in various confined geometries.
main_problem: Determining whether confinement-induced charge redistribution and deviations from local electroneutrality are driven by local geometric curvature or global topological constraints.
main_result: The paper establishes a universal hierarchy of electroneutrality violations based on domain topology, where deviations are strongest in spherical cavities, weaker in cylinders, and weakest in planar slits.
method: Analytical solution of the Poisson-Boltzmann equation using a product manifold representation of the confining domains.
model_or_system: Electrolyte solutions confined within spherical, cylindrical, and planar geometries, modeled using mean-field Poisson-Boltzmann theory with point-ion approximations.
key_observables: Electroneutrality deviation ratio (eta), local charge density, and electrostatic potential.
important_parameters: Reduced cavity size (R/lambda_D), electrolyte concentration, surface charge density, and domain topology (compactness/connectivity).
main_assumptions: Uses mean-field Poisson-Boltzmann theory and assumes a point-ion model, neglecting ion-size effects and explicit ion-ion correlations.
figures_summary: A logarithmic plot showing the deviation ratio (eta) versus reduced cavity size (R/lambda_D) for spherical, cylindrical, and planar geometries, demonstrating a monotonic decrease and a clear topological hierarchy.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the problem of charge redistribution in confinement, introduces a mathematical framework using product manifolds, defines the electroneutrality deviation ratio, presents the results showing the topological hierarchy, and concludes by linking these findings to phenomena like overcharging.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Electroneutrality deviation ratio η(R) = (σHi(R) + σ0)/σ0 for planar slits (Ωslit ≃R2 × [0, δ]), infinite cylindrical shells (Ω≃S1 × R × [0, δ]), and spherical shells (Ω≃S2 × [0, δ]), shown as a function of the reduced cavity size R/λD (logarithmic scale). Although η(R) →0 as R →∞, finite-size deviations follow a topology-controlled hierarchy, spherical > cylindrical > planar. The dotted line indicates η(R) = 0.
